The **2018 Winter Paralympics** ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 2018년 동계 패럴림픽; [RR](/wiki/Revised_Romanization_of_Korean "Revised Romanization of Korean"): *2018nyeon Donggye Paereollimpik*), the 12th [Paralympic Winter Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Winter_Games "Paralympic Winter Games"), and also more generally known as the **PyeongChang 2018 Paralympic Winter Games**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for [athletes with disabilities](/wiki/Disabled_sports "Disabled sports") governed by the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC), that was held in [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang_County "Pyeongchang County"), South Korea, from 9 to 18 March 2018. They were the second Paralympics to be held in South Korea, following the [1988 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1988_Summer_Paralympics "1988 Summer Paralympics") in Seoul.

569 athletes representing a record 49 [National Paralympic Committees](/wiki/National_Paralympic_Committee "National Paralympic Committee") participated in these Games, including 3 newcomers Georgia, North Korea and Tajikistan. Following its debut as disciplines under the Alpine programme in Sochi, snowboarding was expanded into a separate sport with 8 additional events.

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Bids for the 2018 Winter Olympics")

As part of a formal agreement between the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") and the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") first established in 2001,[[1]](#cite_note-bbc-firstglobal-1) the winner of the bid for the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") was also to host the 2018 Winter Paralympics.

Pyeongchang was elected as host during the [123rd IOC Session](/wiki/123rd_IOC_Session "123rd IOC Session") in [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") in 2011, earning the required majority of at least 48 votes in the first round of voting.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-bidresults-3)

* Russia had been suspended by the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") due to the discovery of a [state-sponsored doping program](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") active during the 2014 Winter Olympics and Paralympics.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The IPC allowed Russian athletes to qualify as neutral participants. They fielded 30 athletes in 5 sports, participating as [Neutral Paralympic Athletes](/wiki/Neutral_Paralympic_Athletes_at_the_2018_Winter_Paralympics "Neutral Paralympic Athletes at the 2018 Winter Paralympics") (NPA), marching under the Paralympic flag at the Opening and Closing Ceremonies and the Paralympic anthem played in any ceremony.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* Four more nations competed in Pyeongchang than at the previous winter games. Georgia, North Korea and Tajikistan all made their Winter Paralympics debut,[[19]](#cite_note-19) while Hungary fielded a team after not competing in 2014. Tajikistan is the only country who had sent an athlete to these Games but not the 2018 Winter Olympics.
* In total, 133 female athletes competed at the event.[[20]](#cite_note-record_number_of_female_athletes_paralympics_2022-20)

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

Television rights were sold in various countries and territories; the IPC partnered with the IOC's streaming service [Olympic Channel](/wiki/Olympic_Channel "Olympic Channel") for online streaming coverage of these Paralympics.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2017, the [European Broadcasting Union](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union") renewed its rights to the Paralympics in 25 European countries through 2020
[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the United States, [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Olympic_broadcasts "NBC Olympic broadcasts") announced plans to air nearly twice as much coverage on linear television as it did in Sochi, totaling 250 hours,[[26]](#cite_note-26) along with extensive online streaming coverage.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In Canada, the [CBC](/wiki/CBC_Olympic_broadcasts "CBC Olympic broadcasts") announced that it would broadcast over 600 hours of coverage in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") across its platforms, including [CBC Television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television"), [Ici Radio-Canada Télé](/wiki/Ici_Radio-Canada_T%C3%A9l%C3%A9 "Ici Radio-Canada Télé"), and sublicence partners [Sportsnet One](/wiki/Sportsnet_One "Sportsnet One") and [AMI-tv](/wiki/AMI-tv "AMI-tv").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") returned as the Games' rightsholder in the United Kingdom, planning over 100 hours of television coverage on Channel 4 and [4seven](/wiki/4seven "4seven").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Tickets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tickets")]

Ticket prices for the 2018 Winter Paralympics were announced on 8 June 2017 and tickets went on sale on 21 August 2017.[[30]](#cite_note-ticketprices-30)

Prices for sporting event tickets range from [₩](/wiki/South_Korean_won "South Korean won")10,000 to 50,000 (approx. $8–45 USD). Opening and closing ceremony tickets range from ₩10,000 to ₩140,000 (approx. $8–125 USD).[[30]](#cite_note-ticketprices-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

As of 19 January, tickets to the Paralympic Games were 70% sold. (155,000 tickets out of a total of 223,353 allocated).[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Marketing")]

### Emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Emblem")]

The emblem for the 2018 Winter Paralympics was unveiled on 29 October 2013 at the [National Museum of Korea](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Korea "National Museum of Korea"). It incorporates stylized renditions of the [hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") letter [ㅊ](/wiki/%E3%85%8A "ㅊ") *ch* (as also used in the Olympic emblem), which symbolizes part of the name *Pyeongchang* and resembles ice crystals. The Paralympic emblem features two of these letters joined together, symbolizing equality and a "grand" festival welcoming international athletes and spectators.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mascot")]

Main article: [Soohorang and Bandabi](/wiki/Soohorang_and_Bandabi "Soohorang and Bandabi")

The official mascots of the 2018 Winter Olympics and Paralympics were unveiled on 2 June 2016. The Paralympic mascot, Bandabi, is an [Asian black bear](/wiki/Asian_black_bear "Asian black bear") that symbolizes "strong will and courage".[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Cultural events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cultural events")]

To attract interest from residents and foreign tourists, the [Korea Tourism Organization](/wiki/Korea_Tourism_Organization "Korea Tourism Organization") organized *Snow Festival*, a "[Hallyu](/wiki/Korean_Wave "Korean Wave") festival", to serve as cultural programming for the Paralympics. Actors [Jang Keun-suk](/wiki/Jang_Keun-suk "Jang Keun-suk") and [Lee Dong-wook](/wiki/Lee_Dong-wook "Lee Dong-wook") purchased 2,018 and 1,000 tickets for themselves and fans to attend meetups at para ice hockey games, while a [K-pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") concert featuring [B1A4](/wiki/B1A4 "B1A4") and [BtoB](/wiki/BtoB_(band) "BtoB (band)") was also organized.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Concerns and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Concerns and controversies")]

### North Korean relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Winter_Paralympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: North Korean relations")]

See also: [North Korea–South Korea relations](/wiki/North_Korea%E2%80%93South_Korea_relations "North Korea–South Korea relations") and [North Korea at the 2018 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/North_Korea_at_the_2018_Winter_Paralympics "North Korea at the 2018 Winter Paralympics")

Prior to the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") agreed to have its athletes march together with those of the South Korean team during the opening ceremonies, and field a unified women's hockey team. However, during a meeting in Pyeongchang between the leaders of their National Paralympic Committees, the two countries were unable to organize a similar arrangement for the Paralympics. The South Korean Paralympic Committee stated that North Korean officials had requested that the [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks "Liancourt Rocks") (which are the subject of an [ongoing sovereignty dispute](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks_dispute "Liancourt Rocks dispute") between South Korea and Japan) be included on the [Korean Unification Flag](/wiki/Korean_Unification_Flag "Korean Unification Flag") during the Paralympics. South Korea declined this request, as they considered it contradictory to IPC recommendations against political gestures.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

IPC president [Andrew Parsons](/wiki/Andrew_Parsons_(sports_administrator) "Andrew Parsons (sports administrator)") expressed disappointment over the decision, but noted that the country "respects and values the IPC's vision and mission" and had "committed to working further with the IPC to improve the lives of people with an impairment in North Korea", while also acknowledging that the IOC had "made great progress in opening up dialogue between the two nations" prior to the Olympics, and that their meeting "underlines the tremendous ability of sport to bring countries together in positive dialogue."[[44]](#cite_note-44)
